title: Introducing Mary Midgley and what is philosophy for?
text:
hello
my name is claire mccool and i'm a
lecturer in philosophy at durham
university
hi my name is rachel wiseman i'm a
lecturer in philosophy
at university of liverpool i'm a
recipient of the british academy
rising star engagement award for some
work that i'm doing with rachel
so the british academy is a fellowship
of scholars and researchers and they fun
research
in the humanities and social sciences
and claire and i have been really lucky
that they've supported our research on
a group of four amazing women
philosophers for several years now
yes so the women that we're looking at
are elizabeth anscombe
philippa foot iris murdoch and mary
midgley and we're actually trying to
write a book about their lives and their
philosophy
and we're trying to argue that actually
what we have here is a
philosophical school that hasn't really
been recognized yet
but it's a bit tricky uh trying to write
together in this lockdown period
yeah so one of the things that's really
helped us to write together
is um this books that we share so we've
got a number of books in common
um and we talk about those books all the
time so in a way
the books that inspire of us have been
bringing us together
during the lockdown and we're going to
talk to you today or show you today two
of those books that have been especially
inspirational
and they're both books by mary mitchelly
yes so the first one
is this what is philosophy for so
mary wrote this when she was 98 and it
was published
just before she died in 2018 and when
she was 99
um and this is these our copies are
particularly special to us because
they're inscribed
so there's mine and rachel's is
yeah mine says rachel wise and then gets
what is philosophy for because she's a
very helpful girl
really helpful um
what is philosophy for why is it
do you necessarily so i was just gonna
say this book so inspiring in lockdown
because i think one of the things that
when you're a philosopher
um people you feel like what you're
doing is maybe not really connecting to
reality
and i think when the world's in a kind
of upside down state as it is in the
moment it can feel really indulgent to
be reading philosophy books
and in a way this book is a little
manifesto that says
no it's not it's really really important
what you're doing
yeah and mary tells us you know what a
philosopher should do
like what is the job of a philosopher
and she writes
just give her a few words here the
philosopher's business is not
as some people mistakenly think merely
to look inward it is to organize what
concerns everybody
philosophy aims to bring together those
aspects of life that have not yet been
properly connected
so as to make a more coherent more
workable world picture
and that coherent world picture is not a
private luxury
it's something we all need for our lives
um
so she has this idea um that philosophy
isn't luxury it's something that you
have to do and when she's elucidating
this and developing this idea she comes
with this incredible image she says that
philosophers are a bit like plumbers
um and the reason she thinks this is
that
you know our conceptual sk our concept
our conceptual schemes um are very
elaborate systems a bit like
plumbing systems are very elaborate but
we don't tend to notice them and she
thinks that philosophers
need to be able to sort of sniff out and
work out when a conceptual scheme
is no longer working properly and
philosophers need to do the really hard
intellectual work of pulling up the
floorboards and trying to work out
where the blockage is where the
conceptual scheme isn't working and like
disconnect it
where it's not working and then
incredibly inspiringly
do you want to say something about the
inspirational bit well i mean it's
already inspirational i suppose what you
just said claire like
when we're living in times like this
this idea that the philosopher has a
really important role to play in
working out what's going wrong where all
this chaos is coming from
um but yeah then the really exciting bit
is that mary thinks that
philosophers need to come up with new
visions um
that can replace the bad ones that are
causing all this chaos and come
somehow be things that can kind of weave
our lives back together again
um and for this she thinks that we need
help from poets
um so one of the things that claire and
i have been really inspired with in this
book
is how we might have conversations with
poets and other creative people and i
think during lockdown
that's been something that's come up a
lot isn't it i mean people have really
turned to poetry so i think we can see
that mary was sort of really on to
something there
yeah i just mentioned really briefly
that we're running a commemorative
project for mary
um it's called notes from a biscuit tin
and that's the website address
and we'll show it to you at the end of
this video um and
that project involves bringing together
philosophers and poets
um to bring them together to have
conversations around themes from mary
midgie's work
and it's actually inspired by um our
meetings with mary so we used to go and
meet mary
for tea or coffee every now and again
since 2015 we went for about three years
um and we talked to her about philosophy
and have biscuits and tea
and um so we're trying to encourage
these conversations between philosophers
and poets over
uh sharing biscuits and in fact well
it's a little bit
mad but we've sent mary mitchell's
biscuit in on around the world tour
um and that is the kind of material
anchor for these conversations
that we're having um and in a way what
we're trying to do there is
kind of create a different sort of
structure or way of thinking about how
we might do
philosophy together collaboratively um
and that again follows mary's vision
that
philosophy is actually partly creative
discipline
and we're also trying to kind of partly
replicate um the kinds of conversations
that we imagine might have taken place
between the four
women that are the subject of our book
so this is the second book that we
thought we'd share with you uh that's
inspired us
it's mary's autobiography the other from
nirvan here's a lovely picture of her
unlocked down on a boat
um so yeah one of the things about our
research i suppose from the very
beginning
is that we've been really interested in
the the stories of the women and their
personal biographies and we've been
thinking about that a lot in relation to
them as women
and what it's like to be a woman in
philosophy and we felt that
telling the story of their lives would
be a really inspiring thing for other
women who were doing philosophy but
actually returning to
those biographies and math wise and in
particular the out of minerva
in the context of the lockdown and
coronavirus
i think he's really brought those that
historical dimension to life in new ways
um so listening to mary talk about
the disruption of the second world war
and the way that every aspect of their
lives and relationships were kind of
unearthed by that and thinking about
what it is to do philosophy in a time
when the future
and the present are kind of scary and
uncertain
it's been sort of really inspiring and
exciting
and i don't know if you agree with that
claire yeah i do and like there's
certain ideas as well that um
that seem more pertinent than ever which
hopefully we'll be able to bring them
out
when we manage to write this book
finally and but
how about we give mary the last word
yeah that's it a couple of lines
actually did the last few lines from
this book what is philosophy for so she
writes
i suspect that philosophical reasoning
will now become rather
important so she's writing these words
in 2018
we shall need to think about how best to
think about new
and difficult topics how to imagine them
how to visualize them
how to fit them into convincing world
picture and if we don't do that for
ourselves
it's hard to see who will be able to do
it for us
you
